lorehq
─[2]-Primitives──

─[3]-Guides──
CommandsCustomizationGuidesPlatform SupportWorking Across Repos

Active Work Architecture
─[ Rethinking Agents and Skills ]─ ┐

Rethinking Agents and Skills

Core Thesis

Skills and agents serve fundamentally different purposes. The current framework conflates them by deriving agents from skill clustering — but that relationship may be backwards or nonexistent.

Skills: Persistent Tactical Knowledge

Skills are field-earned know-how. They capture:

  • API workarounds, auth quirks, encoding gotchas
  • Non-obvious parameter tricks
  • Platform-specific failure modes and mitigations

They are the core value of Lore — accumulated experience that makes interacting with specific parts of the environment efficient. Skills are persistent and reusable across any context.

Agents: Ephemeral Delegated Context Windows

An agent is a new context window spun up for a task. The orchestrator decides:

  1. This task needs delegation (complexity, domain expertise, context isolation)
  2. What skills are relevant to the problem domain
  3. What conventions, project context, and knowledge to inject
  4. What model is best suited for the work

The agent is equipped on creation — not defined by a static file. It’s born from usefulness, not from skill accumulation.

The Key Insight

There is no natural derivation from skills to agents. An agent isn’t “the thing that owns these skills.” An agent is a delegation decision — the orchestrator saying “let me spawn a worker, load it with what it needs, and point it at this problem.”

  • Skills are what you know (persistent)
  • Agents are who you send (ephemeral, task-scoped)

The Platform Constraint

Most AI coding tools load agents from static files at session start:

  • Claude Code: .claude/agents/*.md — loaded at init, referenced by name in Task tool
  • Cursor: agent definitions are static
  • OpenCode: similar pattern

This means agents can’t be truly ephemeral today. The orchestrator can’t say “create an agent on the fly with these skills and this context” — it has to reference a pre-defined agent file.

Is This Actually Blocking?

Partially. Consider what the static agent file actually provides:

What the file definesCould be dynamic instead?
Name / descriptionYes — orchestrator knows what it’s delegating
Model preferenceYes — orchestrator can choose per-task
Skills listYes — orchestrator can inject relevant skills
Operating rulesYes — these are in docs/context/ already
Domain boundaryYes — this is just the task scope

The static file is a template, not a necessity. The real constraint is the Task tool’s subagent_type parameter — it requires a pre-registered agent name.

Workaround Today

The orchestrator already has the Task tool with subagent_type and a free-form prompt. It can:

  1. Use a generic agent type (or the general-purpose type)
  2. Pack the prompt with: relevant skills, conventions, task scope
  3. Effectively create an “ephemeral agent” via prompt engineering

The static agent file just pre-packages this. It’s a convenience, not an architecture.

What Would Change

If agents become ephemeral delegation decisions:

  • No more .lore/agents/ directory (or it becomes optional templates)
  • No more agent registry
  • Skills get better metadata (tags, domains) so the orchestrator can select them
  • The orchestrator’s delegation logic becomes the “agent creation” — choosing skills, context, model, and scope per-task
  • Agent “templates” could still exist for common delegation patterns

If skills stay as-is but agents decouple:

  • Skills keep their current format and persistence
  • Domain field on skills becomes a tag for selection, not an ownership relationship
  • The type: command distinction (framework vs gotcha) stays useful
  • Skills registry becomes the primary registry; agent registry dissolves

Decision: Hybrid Model — Orchestrator-Curated Worker Agent

The Model

One framework-owned agent: lore-worker-agent. The orchestrator spawns it per-task with a curated prompt containing:

  1. The task itself
  2. Specific skill files to load (orchestrator picks from skills-registry.md)
  3. Specific conventions to follow (if applicable)
  4. Repo path and scope boundaries

The worker loads what it’s told, executes, returns results. It doesn’t get the full session banner — it’s a focused executor, not a second orchestrator.

Why Hybrid

  • Orchestrator curates — it has full conversation context, knows why it’s delegating, knows what already failed. Best position to select skills.
  • Worker executes — fresh context window, no baggage. Best position to do focused work.
  • Fallback — if the worker hits something unexpected, it can self-serve from skills-registry.md.

System Shape

.lore/agents/
├── lore-worker-agent.md    ← framework-owned, one generic worker
└── <operator-agents>.md    ← operator-owned, optional, their rules
  • Framework provides one agent. Operators create their own as they see fit.
  • lore-create-agent skill becomes guidance for operator agents only.
  • Agent registry shrinks to just operator agents (or disappears if none exist).
  • Skills registry becomes the primary artifact.

Delegation Flow

Orchestrator receives task
  → Decides: delegate or handle directly?
  → If delegate: scan skills-registry.md for relevant skills
  → Spawn lore-worker-agent with:
      - Task description
      - Skill file paths to load
      - Convention file paths if applicable
      - Scope boundaries
  → Worker loads, executes, returns result
  → Orchestrator reviews result, handles capture

For compound tasks with independent branches: spawn multiple workers in parallel, each with their own skill set.

What Changes

BeforeAfter
Domain agents derived from skill clusteringOne generic worker, orchestrator curates per-task
Agent registry tracks domain agentsAgent registry tracks operator agents only
lore-create-agent creates domain agentslore-create-agent is operator-only guidance
Skills “belong to” a domain agentSkills are independent, domain is a search tag
Agent captures gotchas itselfWorker reports findings, orchestrator captures
Full session banner on every agentWorker gets only what it needs

Future: Worker Templates

Recurring delegation patterns (e.g., “bash work always needs these 3 skills”) could become lightweight templates — just a skill list shorthand. Not agent files, not a new concept. Revisit when the pattern emerges from actual use.

PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
